skill.name: ios-application-dev
skill.description: Guide for building native iOS apps with UIKit, SnapKit, SwiftUI, Metal, and Apple platform conventions. It covers layout, navigation, accessibility, system integration, coding standards, and design review checklists.
skill.summary: Build and review native iOS interfaces with SwiftUI, UIKit, accessibility, and Apple HIG guidance.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many code execution, weak cryptography, sensitive data, and network indicators. Manual review found these are documentation examples, Swift identifiers, Markdown code fences, or placeholder API URLs, with no executable script, credential exfiltration, prompt injection, or malicious intent found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Examples Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are false positives. The cited locations are Swift code fences or inline API names used for iOS documentation, not Ruby backticks or runnable shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed representative flagged lines show Markdown code fences and Swift API examples. No script file, command runner, shell invocation, or user input execution path is present.

low_findings.title: Placeholder API URL Misclassified as Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are false positives. They appear in Swift networking examples using api.example.com and do not cause the skill itself to make network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are in explanatory Swift snippets and use the reserved example.com domain. The skill has no executable network client or data collection logic.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Keywords Misclassified
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings are false positives. The cited lines use words such as critical, hash-like UI terms, or Metal data type guidance, not cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed flagged areas describe iOS design and Metal rendering concepts. No MD5, SHA1, DES, insecure random generation, or crypto implementation was found in those contexts.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Credential Terms Used in UI Form Example
low_findings.description: The sensitive data findings are false positives. The password references show a SecureField example for an account form and do not include real credentials or key material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The snippet demonstrates a standard password input control. It contains no certificate, private key, token, or embedded secret value.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Keywords Misclassified
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings are false positives. The cited language describes iOS system UI, safe areas, gestures, and device layout guidance, not host inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are design checklist and navigation guidance. There are no calls to inspect the local host, enumerate files, read environment data, or fingerprint the system.

content.user_title: Build Native iOS Apps With SwiftUI and UIKit
content.value_statement: iOS teams need consistent platform guidance across layout, navigation, accessibility, and Swift code quality. This skill provides practical checklists and reference patterns for building native Apple experiences.
actual_capabilities: Guides SwiftUI and UIKit layout decisions using safe areas, touch targets, and adaptive spacing.
Reviews navigation patterns for tab bars, navigation stacks, modals, sheets, and state preservation.
Provides accessibility guidance for Dynamic Type, VoiceOver labels, contrast, motion, and alternate gestures.
Explains system integration patterns for permissions, location, share sheets, lifecycle, haptics, and Spotlight.
Summarizes Swift coding standards for optionals, protocols, concurrency, access control, memory, and errors.
Includes Metal shader and graphics guidance for animation, layers, gradients, GPU data types, and performance.
limitations: It is a guidance skill, not a complete app generator or build system.
It does not replace testing on real iPhone and iPad devices.
It does not validate App Store policy compliance for a specific submission.
It may need updates when Apple changes platform APIs or design guidance.

use_cases.title: Plan a New iOS Feature
use_cases.description: Turn a feature idea into native layout, navigation, permission, and accessibility requirements before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Review a Pull Request
use_cases.description: Check SwiftUI or UIKit changes for platform conventions, Dynamic Type behavior, safe areas, and common anti-patterns.

use_cases.title: Improve Design Handoff
use_cases.description: Convert interface mockups into practical iOS implementation notes that respect Apple conventions and device constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic iOS Layout
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a screen design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this iOS screen idea for touch target size, safe area use, spacing, and Dynamic Type support. List the most important fixes first.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Navigation Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Given this app structure, recommend the right iOS navigation pattern. Compare TabView, NavigationStack, sheets, and alerts for this flow.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Accessibility Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this SwiftUI or UIKit implementation for VoiceOver, Dynamic Type, color contrast, Reduce Motion, and alternative input paths.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production Implementation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an implementation plan for this iOS feature using SwiftUI or UIKit. Include state management, permissions, error handling, accessibility, tests, and review checkpoints.

output_examples.input: Review a profile edit screen with a save button, avatar picker, and account fields.
output: Primary issues: place Save in the navigation confirmation area or bottom thumb zone, keep fields above the keyboard, and add clear validation messages.
Accessibility checks: label the avatar button, support Dynamic Type in form rows, and keep all controls at least 44 points tall.
System behavior: use a sheet for image selection and preserve unsaved input if the app backgrounds.

output_examples.input: Plan navigation for a shopping app with Home, Search, Cart, Orders, and Profile.
output: Use a five-item tab bar for the main sections and preserve navigation state inside each tab.
Use navigation stacks for product detail screens and sheets for scoped tasks such as filters or address editing.
Avoid drawer navigation because it hides primary destinations on iOS.

output_examples.input: Assess a Swift networking feature before code review.
output: Check that async functions handle cancellation, typed errors, decoding failures, and main-thread UI updates.
Confirm user-facing errors are localized and no force unwraps are used for network data.
Add tests for success, server error, invalid response, and cancellation paths.

best_practices: Start with Apple platform conventions before adding custom interaction patterns.
Review accessibility, Dynamic Type, Dark Mode, and safe areas during implementation, not only at release.
Test important flows on small iPhones, large iPhones, iPad layouts, and assistive settings.
anti_patterns: Do not hide primary navigation in drawer menus or gesture-only controls.
Do not request permissions at launch before the user understands the value.
Do not hardcode fixed fonts, colors, or dimensions that break accessibility and Dark Mode.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete iOS app?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, review criteria, and implementation patterns for native iOS development.

faq.question: Does it support both SwiftUI and UIKit?
faq.answer: Yes. The references cover SwiftUI, UIKit, SnapKit layout, system integration, and Swift coding standards.

faq.question: Can it help with accessibility reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Dynamic Type, VoiceOver labels, contrast, Reduce Motion, touch targets, and alternate interactions.

faq.question: Is this useful for code review?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes checklists for navigation, layout, permissions, concurrency, memory, errors, and access control.

faq.question: Does it replace Apple documentation?
faq.answer: No. It summarizes practical guidance, but teams should verify current Apple APIs and App Store requirements.

faq.question: Can it guide Metal or animation work?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes references for Core Animation, gradients, shapes, Metal shaders, and GPU performance considerations.
